stimulus: In her 2005 study Patios and Polyphonies, musicologist Lucia Ferreira-Mendes draws her analysis in part on field recordings of street duets sung in the 1940s in São Paulo by the singers Dora Beltrão and Ines de Sousa. Ferreira-Mendes focuses on how Beltrão and de Sousa improvised the lyrics — replacing the printed verses with pointed commentary on neighborhood politics and sly puns. Ferreira-Mendes’s work was particularly time-consuming because, in order to transcribe the lyrics as Beltrão and de Sousa sang them, she had to play the wax-cylinder recordings repeatedly, since the recordings were quite noisy.
stem: What does the text most strongly suggest about the duets sung by Beltrão and de Sousa?

answerOptions.content: The duets grew in popularity in the decades after they were first sung.
There were few, if any, reliable transcriptions of Beltrão’s and de Sousa’s improvised lyrics when Ferreira-Mendes began her study.
According to Ferreira-Mendes, the duets sung by Beltrão were typically more musically inventive than those sung by de Sousa.
More wax-cylinder recordings were made of Beltrão’s duets than of de Sousa’s.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The text describes the transcription work as particularly time-consuming and explains that Ferreira-Mendes had to play noisy wax-cylinder recordings repeatedly to capture the improvised lyrics. That painstaking approach implies that reliable transcriptions of those lyrics were not otherwise available to her.

Choice A is incorrect because the text never addresses the duets’ popularity over time. Choice C is incorrect because the text treats the two singers in parallel and doesn’t compare their inventiveness. Choice D is incorrect because the text never compares the number of recordings made of each singer.
